file_name: EMERALDHEALTHTHERAPEUTICSINC_06_10_2020-EX-4.5-CONSULTING AGREEMENT - DR. GAETANO MORELLO N.D. INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: Emerald shall not be obliged to deduct or retain from the Fees due to the Contractor, nor shall it be obliged to remit same to the required governmental authority, any amounts that may be required by law or regulation to be deducted, retained and remitted including, without limitation, Federal and Provincial or State Income Tax, Workers' Compensation and Pension Plan deductions and remittances. All such remittances and other payments are entirely the responsibility of the Contractor and the Contractor hereby indemnifies and saves Emerald and its Board members and officers harmless from any liability of any kind whatsoever that they may incur as a result of the Contractor's failure to make such remittances or payments.
perturbation.changed_text: Emerald shall endeavor not to deduct or retain from the Fees due to the Contractor, nor shall it be obliged to remit same to the required governmental authority, any amounts that may be required by law or regulation to be deducted, retained and remitted including, without limitation, Federal and Provincial or State Income Tax, Workers' Compensation and Pension Plan deductions and remittances. All such remittances and other payments are entirely the general responsibility of the Contractor, and the Contractor hereby indemnifies and saves Emerald and its Board members and officers harmless from any liability of any kind whatsoever that they may incur as a result of the Contractor's general failure to make such remittances or payments.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall not be obliged" to "shall endeavor not to" introduces ambiguity regarding Emerald's responsibility. Replacing "entirely the responsibility" with "entirely the general responsibility" weakens the clear assignment of obligation. Replacing "Contractor's failure" with "Contractor's general failure" further obscures the responsibility. This creates a legal ambiguity regarding who is ultimately responsible for ensuring proper deductions and remittances.
perturbation.law_citation: Income Tax Act, RSC 1985, c. 1 (5th Supp), s. 153
perturbation.law_explanation: The Income Tax Act (Canada), section 153, mandates employers to deduct and remit income tax from payments made to employees and contractors. By stating that Emerald 'shall endeavor not to' deduct, retain, or remit, the modified text implies that Emerald may not fully comply with its legal obligation to deduct and remit income tax, creating a direct contradiction. The change regarding responsibility for failure creates more uncertainty.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall not be obliged" to "shall endeavor not to" introduces ambiguity regarding Emerald's responsibility to deduct and remit taxes, potentially contradicting the Income Tax Act, which mandates these deductions. While a specific snippet from the Income Tax Act isn't provided, the Law Explanation explicitly states that section 153 of the Act requires employers to deduct and remit income tax. By weakening the obligation, the modified text creates a legal contradiction under the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, making it difficult to enforce the original clear obligation. The alteration from 'entirely the responsibility' to 'entirely the general responsibility' and 'Contractor's failure' to 'Contractor's general failure' also contributes to this weakening of the obligation and increasing ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor will devote sufficient time and attention to the business and affairs of Emerald to provide the Services, use his or her best efforts to promote the interests of Emerald, and will carry out his or her Services honestly, in good faith and in the best interests of Emerald.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor will generally devote time and attention to the business and affairs of Emerald to provide the Services, use reasonable efforts to promote the interests of Emerald, and will endeavor to carry out his or her Services in a generally acceptable manner, in what he or she believes to be the interests of Emerald.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "sufficient time and attention" to "generally devote time and attention" it becomes unclear how much time is actually required. Replacing "best efforts" with "reasonable efforts" lowers the standard of performance required. Substituting "honestly, in good faith and in the best interests" with "in a generally acceptable manner, in what he or she believes to be the interests" significantly weakens the obligation, introducing subjectivity and removing the explicit requirement for good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Fiduciary Duty - common law principle in Canada
perturbation.law_citation: Peoples Department Stores Inc. (Trustee of) v. Wise, 2004 SCC 68
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Contractor is an independent contractor and not an employee or director, the clause attempts to define the standard of care he owes to Emerald. By replacing 'best efforts' with 'reasonable efforts,' and introducing subjectivity ('in what he or she believes'), the modified text contradicts the common law principle of fiduciary duty (as articulated in cases like Peoples Department Stores Inc. v. Wise), which requires directors and officers (and, by extension, those in positions of trust) to act honestly, in good faith, and with a view to the best interests of the corporation. The modified text creates a lower, more ambiguous standard that could allow the contractor to prioritize their own interests over Emerald's, provided they deem it 'reasonable'.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care, potentially contradicting the requirement for good faith and acting in the best interests of Emerald. The perturbation explanation notes the shift from 'best efforts' to 'reasonable efforts,' and the substitution of 'honestly, in good faith and in the best interests' with a more subjective standard. While no law snippets were provided, the law explanation suggests a potential conflict with the principle of fiduciary duty, which requires acting in good faith and in the best interests of the corporation. The modified contract language introduces subjectivity, which weakens the original obligation, making it difficult to enforce the initial standard of care.
